skill.name: iterative-runner
skill.description: Runs Claude in a retry loop until tests pass or task completes. Use for TDD loops, overnight builds, or any task needing repeated iteration until success. Triggers on: loop until done, keep trying, retry until pass, TDD loop, iterate until tests pass.
skill.summary: Run repeated Claude sessions until tests pass, blockers are documented, or a defined completion marker appears.
skill.author: BarisSozen

security_audit.summary: The external command findings are partially true positives because the skill recommends repeated Claude CLI execution, including a persistent shell loop. The weak cryptography and network reconnaissance findings are false positives at the cited lines; no cryptographic algorithm, network scan, or exfiltration behavior is present. Publication is acceptable with a warning about cost, resource use, and controlled iteration limits.

medium_findings.title: Repeated Claude CLI Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to run Claude repeatedly until a completion condition is reached. This is legitimate for test automation, but it can consume tokens, modify files across iterations, and continue undesired work if limits are not enforced.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell loop and iterative-runner examples are visible in the cited lines. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the skill also tells users to define success criteria and set iteration limits.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Dismissed
low_findings.description: The cited description line contains only a natural language summary of the skill. No weak cryptographic algorithm or cryptographic operation is present at this location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is front matter text describing retry behavior. I found no evidence of cryptographic code or algorithm names there.

low_findings.title: Network Reconnaissance Finding Dismissed
low_findings.description: The cited line is a use case for test-driven development loops. It does not contain network scanning commands, host discovery, or network access instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 12 only mentions TDD loops. I found no evidence of network reconnaissance behavior in the inspected file.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Findings Are Mostly Benign
low_findings.description: Several static external-command detections correspond to Markdown examples, inline flags, or a prompt template. The only confirmed command risk is the intentional Claude retry loop documented in the medium finding.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain Markdown formatting and completion criteria text, not executable implementation code. They are safe by themselves, but they support the broader retry workflow.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent Shell Loop Around Claude
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented loop repeatedly invokes the Claude CLI without an internal stop condition. Users must add external limits to avoid runaway cost or unintended repeated changes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited command is a direct infinite loop pattern around a CLI invocation. The file later recommends max iterations, which reduces but does not remove the operational risk.

content.value_statement: Long coding tasks often fail because each attempt stops before tests pass. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code repeat work against clear completion criteria and bounded iteration limits.
actual_capabilities: Defines when to use repeated agent runs for TDD and complex implementation work.
Shows how to set completion criteria before starting an iterative task.
Recommends max iteration limits to reduce runaway execution risk.
Provides example prompts for test-first implementation loops.
Includes a reusable task template with success criteria and blocker reporting.
limitations: Does not provide the iterative-runner executable or installation steps.
Requires users to supply clear tests, lint commands, or completion markers.
Can increase token use and runtime when tasks are broad or underspecified.
Does not verify that generated changes are correct beyond user-defined criteria.

use_cases.title: Test-Driven Feature Work
use_cases.description: Use bounded iterations to write failing tests, implement the feature, and repeat until the test suite passes.

use_cases.title: Overnight Build Repair
use_cases.description: Run a limited retry cycle that fixes build failures and reports blockers when the task cannot finish.

use_cases.title: Complex Refactor Completion
use_cases.description: Break a refactor into clear phases and keep the agent focused on defined success criteria.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Retry Task
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt for a small task with one completion marker.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use iterative-runner to complete this task: [task]. Stop when [success condition] is true. Use at most 10 iterations and report any blocker.

prompt_templates.title: TDD Implementation Loop
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate prompt for test-first feature development.
prompt_templates.prompt: Implement [feature] with test-driven development. First add a failing test, then implement the smallest passing change. Repeat until all related tests pass.

prompt_templates.title: Build Failure Recovery
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate prompt for repeated CI or local build repair.
prompt_templates.prompt: Fix the current build failure. After each attempt, run the agreed build check. Stop after 15 iterations or when the build succeeds.

prompt_templates.title: Bounded Refactor Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt for multi-phase work with blocker reporting.
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor [component] in phases. Define success criteria for each phase, run checks after each change, and document blockers after repeated failures.

output_examples.input: Iterate on a failing authentication test until it passes.
output_examples.output: The agent reports the failing test, describes the implementation changes, confirms the test result, and states whether the completion marker was reached.

output_examples.input: Run a bounded loop to repair a broken build.
output_examples.output: The final response lists attempted fixes, the last build status, remaining blockers, and the iteration count used.

output_examples.input: Complete a small refactor with lint and tests as success criteria.
output_examples.output: The result summarizes changed areas, confirms lint and test status, and identifies any criteria that still need human review.

best_practices: Set a maximum iteration count before starting any loop.
Define objective success criteria, such as passing tests or a clear completion marker.
Keep each iterative task narrow enough for the agent to verify progress.
anti_patterns: Do not run unbounded loops on vague goals.
Do not use this skill when no automated check or review criterion exists.
Do not let repeated attempts make unrelated changes across the repository.

faq.question: What does this skill help automate?
faq.answer: It helps run repeated coding attempts until tests pass, a build succeeds, or a completion condition is met.

faq.question: Does it include a runner program?
faq.answer: No. The skill documents the workflow and example commands, but it does not include an executable implementation.

faq.question: Is it safe to run overnight?
faq.answer: Only with strict max iteration limits, clear success criteria, and a repository state you can review afterward.

faq.question: Can it be used with Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow applies to Codex when you provide bounded instructions and objective checks.

faq.question: What should I define before starting?
faq.answer: Define the task, success criteria, max iterations, checks to run, and blocker reporting rules.

faq.question: What is the main risk?
faq.answer: The main risk is repeated agent execution that consumes resources or makes unwanted changes if boundaries are weak.
